purpose: Track implementation-level evidence for external projects considered by Complex. Popularity is a discovery signal; transfer requires pinned implementation evidence, upstream validation, explicit rejection boundaries, and Complex-side reproduction or comparison.

status_meanings.discovered: A relevant project was found, but its implementation has not been inspected.
status_meanings.implementation_inspected: Pinned code, configuration, tests, or evaluation assets were inspected; no local reproduction is claimed.
status_meanings.reproduced: A bounded upstream mechanism was run locally with a recorded command and result.
status_meanings.comparatively_evaluated: The reproduced mechanism and the Complex baseline were evaluated on the same task and metrics.
status_meanings.transferred: A bounded mechanism was implemented in Complex with rollback and evaluation evidence.
status_meanings.validated_in_complex: Repeated real transcripts or end-to-end projects show that the transferred mechanism improves Complex outcomes.

selection_rule: Select references by problem fit, implementation legibility, upstream evaluation quality, and transfer cost. Do not select or promote by stars alone.

references.original_goal: Program modular language-model systems and optimize prompts or weights against an explicit metric and development set.
references.original_non_goals: DSPy is not a durable project controller, repository recovery system, or universal substitute for task-specific evaluation design.
references.state_or_data_path: Task examples form a development/train set; a DSPy program consumes each input; a metric scores predictions; optimizers use program, metric, and examples to search candidate instructions, demonstrations, or weights.
references.control_path: Evaluate establishes the baseline and aggregates metric results; an optimizer compile call searches candidates under a budget; the resulting compiled program is re-evaluated on held-out examples.
references.operating_constraints: A meaningful run needs representative examples, a discriminating metric, a baseline, model access, and an explicit call/time budget.
references.failure_boundaries: An optimizer can overfit a weak metric or small development set, consume substantial model calls, and improve benchmark score without improving project governance.
references.artifact_revision_provenance: Implementation inspection is pinned to Git commit 027d9f45f4d60aedadee3fd587d1f4f3584567df; no package artifact was executed.
implementation_evidence: docs/docs/learn/evaluation/overview.md requires an initial development set, a task metric, and a baseline before optimization.
docs/docs/learn/optimization/optimizers.md defines optimizer input as program plus metric plus training inputs and exposes compile-based search over instructions, demonstrations, or weights.
dspy/evaluate/evaluate.py and dspy/evaluate/metrics.py implement evaluation rather than treating prompt wording as self-validating.
upstream_validation: The optimizer guide reports runnable tutorial gains for ReAct and RAG, while also stating that results depend on task data, metric, model calls, and optimization budget.
Upstream tests cover evaluation and optimizer behavior; tutorial gains are not evidence that Complex itself improves.
references.candidate_transfer: Require a development set, metric, baseline, candidate comparison, and rollback before optimizing a reusable Complex prompt contract.
references.rejected: Treating every runtime failure as a prompt defect or requiring a DSPy optimizer for ordinary project beats.
references.not_transferable: DSPy optimization requires task-specific examples, model calls, and budget; its benchmark gains cannot be generalized to project governance.
references.reproduction_result: Not run because meaningful optimizer reproduction needs a project-specific metric, examples, and paid model calls.
references.same_task_comparison: Not run; the required Complex prompt development set and fixed metric do not yet exist.
references.complex_validation_evidence: None; no DSPy mechanism has been transferred into Complex.
references.rollback_route: No runtime dependency was added; remove the candidate record if a task-specific comparison shows no value.
references.refresh_trigger: A stable Complex prompt development set, metric, or materially changed DSPy optimizer implementation becomes available.
references.next_validation: Build a 20-case Complex prompt development set and compare the current prompt contract with at least one candidate under a fixed metric.

references.original_goal: Evaluate and red-team prompts, agents, and RAG systems through declarative provider, test, assertion, metric, and CI configuration.
references.original_non_goals: Promptfoo does not provide a project source of truth, durable multi-beat controller, or proof that output assertions capture overall project quality.
references.state_or_data_path: Configuration expands prompts, providers, and test variables into cases; providers return outputs; assertions produce pass/fail and score records; the evaluator aggregates results for comparison or CI.
references.control_path: The eval command loads configuration, generates a test matrix, invokes or replays providers, evaluates assertions and metrics, then persists and renders results.
references.operating_constraints: Useful comparisons require identical task inputs, stable provider/model settings, outcome-relevant assertions, controlled caching, and separate human review for qualities not captured mechanically.
references.failure_boundaries: String assertions can be gamed or miss repository state and long-horizon outcomes; model-graded assertions add evaluator variance and cost.
references.artifact_revision_provenance: npm package 0.121.19 maps to Git tag 0.121.19 at commit 1ede17aaed940e6dff04f71d24e4ecc011809dae; the local smoke uses that exact package version.
implementation_evidence: examples/compare-agentic-sdks/promptfooconfig.yaml compares multiple agent providers on one codebase with a shared structured task and deterministic metric.
examples/anthropic/claude-code-session/promptfooconfig.yaml combines test variables with model-graded rubrics.
site/docs/providers/echo.md evaluates logged outputs locally without generating new model responses.
upstream_validation: The repository contains deterministic, model-graded, guardrail, red-team, smoke, and configuration tests.
Provider matrices make model or harness comparisons explicit instead of relying on one successful transcript.
references.candidate_transfer: Add optional same-input evaluation matrices for redacted Complex transcripts and project prompts; retain deterministic assertions, outcome metrics, and human review as separate evidence.
references.rejected: Making promptfoo a required runtime or treating assertion pass as proof of project quality.
references.not_transferable: Complex behavior includes repository state and multi-beat outcomes that cannot be reduced to output-string assertions alone.
references.reproduction_result: promptfoo 0.121.19: two zero-cost echo-provider cases passed deterministic assertions; this demonstrates logged-output provider and assertion execution, not candidate-versus-baseline comparison or Complex outcome improvement.
references.same_task_comparison: Not run; the fixture exercises logged-output evaluation but does not compare a Complex baseline and candidate on real transcripts.
references.transfer_artifact: None; only an optional upstream-mechanism fixture and evidence record were added.
references.complex_validation_evidence: None; no real Complex transcript outcome has been attributed to promptfoo evaluation.
references.rollback_route: Remove the optional fixture and keep the existing behavior reviewer if same-task evaluation adds no decision value.
references.refresh_trigger: A redacted transcript set, outcome metric, promptfoo provider change, or contradictory false-positive result appears.
references.next_validation: Run a provider/model matrix on redacted real Complex transcripts and compare correction count, outcome pass, and false-positive marker pass.

references.original_goal: Provide persistent, scoped memory for agents with extraction, storage, update history, retrieval, filtering, and ranking.
references.original_non_goals: The OSS SDK is not an authoritative project evidence ledger, and it does not include every managed-platform temporal or project-memory optimization.
references.state_or_data_path: Conversation or content is scoped by user/agent/run; the pinned V3 add path uses additive extraction, exact-content hashing and history records, while update and delete remain explicit APIs; search retrieves and ranks stored records within configured backends.
references.control_path: Memory.add validates scope and input, extracts additive candidate facts, removes exact-hash duplicates, and persists memory/history records. Memory.update and Memory.delete are separate explicit operations. Memory.search applies the capabilities available in the configured retrieval stack.
references.operating_constraints: Meaningful behavior depends on LLM extraction, embedder/vector-store configuration, scope identifiers, optional graph store, ranking settings, and expiration policy.
references.failure_boundaries: Generated memories may be wrong, duplicate, stale, or improperly scoped; managed benchmark results do not transfer to the OSS path or to evidence authority.
references.artifact_revision_provenance: Implementation inspection is pinned to Git commit 633b0353422ff7634f8ec72daa4d1aed9e224983; no package or managed-service artifact was executed.
implementation_evidence: mem0/memory/main.py at the pinned revision routes the V3 add path through ADDITIVE_EXTRACTION_PROMPT, adds extracted facts, and removes exact-hash duplicates; it does not automatically choose update/delete lifecycle operations.
mem0/configs/prompts.py defines additive extraction separately from update/delete behavior, and tests/memory/test_main.py exercises the pinned add/search/update/delete contracts.
Update and delete are explicit APIs; scope and history provide lifecycle metadata but do not establish evidence authority or semantic contradiction resolution.
upstream_validation: The README links reproducible memory benchmarks but warns that managed-platform optimizations are proprietary and OSS users should not expect identical numbers.
The code and tests expose memory lifecycle behavior more directly than a generic instruction to keep context small.
references.candidate_transfer: Define explicit admission, exact and semantic deduplication, supersession, expiry, history, provenance and retrieval rules for Warm Index material; do not imply that an additive memory path resolves stale or contradictory project facts automatically.
references.rejected: Giving agent-generated memories the same evidentiary authority as verified project facts or using personal-memory semantics as the project source of truth.
references.not_transferable: Managed temporal ranking and proprietary optimizations are not present in the OSS path; project governance needs stricter authority and contradiction rules than personalization memory.
references.reproduction_result: Not run because the meaningful extraction path requires an LLM and storage configuration; the managed benchmark is not equivalent to the OSS SDK.
references.same_task_comparison: Not run; no oversized project-state benchmark currently compares Mem0 lifecycle rules with the Complex Warm Index.
references.complex_validation_evidence: None; no memory lifecycle has been transferred into Complex.
references.rollback_route: Keep the current pointer-based Hot/Warm/Cold model if a versioned memory lifecycle loses authority or recovery fidelity.
references.refresh_trigger: A representative oversized state set, authoritative-memory evaluator, or relevant OSS lifecycle change becomes available.
references.next_validation: Compare a versioned Warm Index memory lifecycle against the current Hot/Warm/Cold pointer model on an oversized real project state.

references.original_goal: Build long-running stateful agents through explicit shared state, graph transitions, durable execution, checkpoints, interrupts, and memory.
references.original_non_goals: LangGraph does not define a project's scientific evidence boundary or guarantee durability without a conformant checkpointer and deployment surface.
references.state_or_data_path: Typed graph state is read by nodes and updated through partial-state reducers; checkpoints persist channel values, pending writes, metadata, and thread-scoped history.
references.control_path: StateGraph registers nodes and conditional edges, compiles the graph, executes transitions with retry/timeout/interrupt behavior, and delegates persistence to the checkpointer interface.
references.operating_constraints: Durability depends on an explicit state schema, reducer semantics, thread identifiers, checkpointer capabilities, and deployment/observability choices outside the graph definition.
references.failure_boundaries: Unsupported checkpoint capabilities, non-idempotent nodes, bad reducers, or incorrect interrupt/retry design can lose, duplicate, or corrupt work.
references.artifact_revision_provenance: Implementation inspection is pinned to Git commit 49ae27c2ae983cfb92091b0dea9f7bc37a716479. The reproduced PyPI artifacts are separately pinned to langgraph 1.2.9, langgraph-checkpoint 4.1.1, and conformance 0.0.2; no source-commit equivalence is claimed.
implementation_evidence: libs/langgraph/langgraph/graph/state.py defines nodes as State to Partial State functions, typed state/context schemas, reducers, edges, branches, retry, timeout, and compilation into an executable graph.
libs/checkpoint-conformance provides required tests for put, put_writes, get_tuple, list, and delete_thread plus optional copy, prune, and history capabilities.
State and checkpoint tests cover interruption, retry, migration, and update semantics.
upstream_validation: The official checkpoint conformance package makes persistence an executable contract rather than a documentation convention.
Production deployment and observability are separate surfaces; the open-source graph alone does not prove an application is durable.
references.candidate_transfer: Give any future Complex controller adapter an explicit state schema, transition table, checkpoint interface, and conformance tests before calling it durable.
references.rejected: Installing LangGraph in every target project or turning routine user questions into interrupts.
references.not_transferable: Complex is also a protocol and Codex skill for repositories that do not run a Python workflow engine.
references.reproduction_command: uv run --with langgraph==1.2.9 --with langgraph-checkpoint==4.1.1 --with langgraph-checkpoint-conformance==0.0.2 python docs/evals/reference-langgraph-checkpoint.py
references.reproduction_result: LangGraph 1.2.9, langgraph-checkpoint 4.1.1, and conformance 0.0.2: InMemorySaver passed all five required capabilities; copy_thread, delete_for_runs, and prune were correctly reported as unsupported.
references.same_task_comparison: Not run; checkpoint conformance has not been compared with the prose-plus-state Complex recovery path on one controller task.
references.transfer_artifact: None; no LangGraph controller adapter is part of Complex.
references.complex_validation_evidence: None; local conformance proves an upstream checkpoint contract only.
references.rollback_route: Keep Complex runtime-agnostic and delete any future adapter if it adds dependency cost without better recovery or stop correctness.
references.refresh_trigger: A minimal controller adapter, recovery benchmark, or checkpoint interface change appears.
references.next_validation: Build a minimal Complex controller state adapter and compare recovery, duplicate work, and stop correctness with the current prose-plus-state approach.

references.original_goal: Continuously turn issue-tracker work into isolated Codex runs with bounded concurrency, reconciliation, retries, and operator-visible status.
references.original_non_goals: Symphony explicitly is not a general-purpose workflow engine and does not model open-ended research evidence or every project portfolio.
references.state_or_data_path: Issue/tracker state and workflow policy produce eligible work; the orchestrator stores running, claimed, blocked, retry, token, poll, and snapshot state; workers write into isolated workspaces.
references.control_path: Poll and refresh tracker state, reconcile running work, dispatch eligible tasks under concurrency limits, observe worker exits, schedule continuation checks or bounded backoff, and expose snapshots.
references.operating_constraints: The reference implementation assumes Elixir, tracker configuration, Codex app-server integration, isolated workspace management, and operator-visible runtime state.
references.failure_boundaries: Tracker mismatch, workspace failure, stale claims, uncontrolled concurrency, or missing reconciliation can duplicate or strand work; coding-ticket assumptions may distort research routing.
references.artifact_revision_provenance: Implementation inspection is pinned to Git commit 4cbe3a9699a73b862466c0b157ceca0c1985d6d7; no Elixir runtime artifact was executed.
implementation_evidence: SPEC.md separates policy, configuration, coordination, execution, integration, and observability layers and defines one authoritative orchestrator state.
elixir/lib/symphony_elixir/orchestrator.ex owns running, claimed, blocked, retry, token, poll, reconciliation, and snapshot state.
Normal worker exit schedules an active-state continuation check; failure uses bounded exponential backoff; reconciliation precedes dispatch.
upstream_validation: The repository includes orchestrator, workspace, dashboard, configuration, snapshot, and live end-to-end tests.
Its specification states explicit non-goals, including not being a general-purpose workflow engine.
references.candidate_transfer: Use one authoritative controller state, stable work identifiers, reconcile-before-dispatch, bounded concurrency, isolated workspaces, continuation checks, and runtime snapshots for executable long-project harnesses.
references.rejected: Requiring an issue tracker, Elixir daemon, or per-issue coding workflow for research and document projects.
references.not_transferable: Symphony's tracker and workspace assumptions fit coding tickets better than open-ended research portfolios.
references.reproduction_result: Not run because the reference implementation requires Elixir, tracker configuration, and Codex app-server integration not present in the Complex repository.
references.same_task_comparison: Not run; no shadow controller has yet run beside a real multi-lane Complex project.
references.complex_validation_evidence: None; Symphony behavior has not been transferred into Complex.
references.rollback_route: Retain the existing lightweight state and lane records if a shadow controller adds orchestration overhead without reducing duplicate work or missed continuation.
references.refresh_trigger: A real multi-lane shadow run, new Codex orchestration surface, or upstream orchestrator change occurs.
references.next_validation: Shadow a lightweight controller state and reconcile loop over one real multi-lane Complex project before choosing a daemon or database.

references.original_goal: Repeatedly run a fresh coding-agent instance until PRD stories pass, preserving memory in Git, prd.json, and progress.txt.
references.original_non_goals: Ralph does not provide independent outcome evaluation, typed durable state, differentiated retry policy, or a general research-project control plane.
references.state_or_data_path: A PRD JSON file holds prioritized stories and acceptance criteria; each fresh agent reads Git and progress.txt, modifies the repository, updates story status and progress, and commits durable results.
references.control_path: The shell loop invokes a new agent process, checks for a completion promise, otherwise advances until a maximum iteration count while external files carry context.
references.operating_constraints: The loop assumes a coding repository, executable stories, command-line agent access, permissive tool settings, and reliable file/Git handoff.
references.failure_boundaries: A self-reported promise or fixed iteration cap can stop incorrectly; undifferentiated retries and permission bypass can repeat failure or create unsafe side effects.
references.artifact_revision_provenance: Implementation inspection is pinned to Git commit 6c53cb0b831ebe8739c6a003e22af14902d8b0b5; no agent wrapper was executed.
implementation_evidence: ralph.sh starts a new Amp or Claude process on each iteration and persists cross-iteration memory in files and Git.
prd.json.example stores prioritized user stories, acceptance criteria, pass status, and notes.
The loop stops on a model-emitted promise marker or a fixed maximum iteration count.
upstream_validation: The implementation is small and auditable, which makes both its useful clean-context pattern and its stop weaknesses visible.
No independent outcome evaluator or retry classification is present in the shell loop.
references.candidate_transfer: Use fresh-context iterations with durable external state and explicit acceptance criteria when context contamination is the main risk.
references.rejected: Permission bypass, fixed-iteration completion, a self-reported promise as the only completion signal, and undifferentiated retries.
references.not_transferable: Ralph is optimized for bounded coding stories and does not provide the evidence, responsibility, or portfolio model required by Complex.
references.reproduction_result: Code path inspected; no agent run was performed because reproducing the wrapper would only confirm its already explicit self-report and max-iteration semantics.
references.same_task_comparison: Not run; a review-lane task with fixed fact ledger and outcome evaluator has not yet compared fresh and same-thread contexts.
references.complex_validation_evidence: None; no Ralph wrapper behavior has been transferred into Complex.
references.rollback_route: Retain same-thread continuation where clean restart loses necessary state or creates more recovery cost than review independence gains.
references.refresh_trigger: A clean-context review experiment, context-contamination failure, or Ralph stop/evaluator change occurs.
references.next_validation: Compare clean-context worker generations against same-thread continuation on a review lane with a fixed fact ledger and outcome evaluator.

references.original_goal: Let a model solve software tasks through a constrained agent-computer interface with configurable tools, history processing, retries, review, budgets, and saved trajectories.
references.original_non_goals: SWE-agent does not define non-software project evidence, public-claim responsibility, or a universal evaluator for research and document work.
references.state_or_data_path: Problem statement, environment state, tool observations, processed history, model outputs, and trajectory records flow through each attempt; RetryAgent retains attempt results for reviewer selection.
references.control_path: Agent setup binds model, tools, environment, hooks, and history; run steps through tool calls under budgets/timeouts; retry resets the environment, stores trajectories, reviews attempts, and selects a result.
references.operating_constraints: Meaningful runs require a task instance, container/image, model and cost budget, compatible tools, timeout settings, and an evaluator or reviewer.
references.failure_boundaries: Benchmark-specific interfaces can overfit software patches; bad environment reset, history policy, or reviewer selection can discard useful work or repeat costly attempts.
references.artifact_revision_provenance: Implementation inspection is pinned to Git commit 1132b3e80a45487ce8423f75d0e180874bf84caa; no benchmark image or model run was executed.
implementation_evidence: sweagent/agent/agents.py separates configuration, tools, history processors, hooks, environment, model, step output, trajectories, and run results.
RetryAgent resets the environment, budgets attempts, stores each trajectory, invokes review logic, and selects a result instead of merely extending one conversation.
Command timeout and format-error paths are explicit in configuration and agent code.
upstream_validation: The repository includes SWE-bench configurations, agent tests, environment fixtures, reviewer logic, and trajectory persistence.
Benchmark performance depends on issue distribution, model, tools, environment image, and cost budget.
references.candidate_transfer: Treat tool interface, history policy, retry attempt, environment reset, reviewer, budget, and trajectory as explicit Harness contracts for important execution lanes.
references.rejected: Copying SWE-bench-specific tools, prompts, or software-only assumptions into every Complex project.
references.not_transferable: Research and governance projects may not have executable tests or patch-based submissions, so their evaluator and artifact contracts differ.
references.reproduction_result: Not run because a meaningful SWE-agent reproduction requires a task image, model budget, and benchmark instance.
references.same_task_comparison: Not run; no bounded Complex code/data task has compared explicit reset/retry/reviewer contracts with the current Codex path.
references.complex_validation_evidence: None; no SWE-agent mechanism has been transferred into Complex.
references.rollback_route: Keep the current Codex execution path if explicit trajectory and reset machinery adds cost without better task outcomes or recovery.
references.refresh_trigger: A bounded executable task, task image/model budget, or relevant upstream retry/reviewer change becomes available.
references.next_validation: Use one bounded code or data task to compare current Codex execution with explicit environment reset, retry-review, and trajectory evidence.

references.original_goal: Provide a local coding agent and app-server protocol whose threads, turns, items, approvals, skills, sandbox policies, plans, and persisted goals are explicit platform surfaces.
references.original_non_goals: Codex does not define Complex standing lanes, project-nature labels, beat_objective, evidence governance, or a universal long-project organization model.
references.state_or_data_path: The app server persists thread, turn, item, instruction-source, goal, approval, token, and runtime-environment state; repository AGENTS.md and skills supply scoped durable instructions.
references.control_path: A client initializes the app server, starts/resumes/forks a thread, starts turns, streams item events, handles approvals, observes turn completion, and uses surface capabilities actually exposed by the current runtime.
references.operating_constraints: Availability differs by Codex App, CLI, IDE, configuration, permission profile, experimental capability, and current tool exposure; repository skills also depend on discovery roots and working-directory semantics.
references.failure_boundaries: Complex can conflict with Codex when it presents its own conventions as platform primitives, promises unavailable UI state, or treats platform resource fit as a user-authority question.
references.artifact_revision_provenance: Implementation inspection is pinned to Git commit aff7c696596797b84c04c6d4c84fbb4e471c2926; experimental app-server fields are not treated as stable across surfaces.
implementation_evidence: codex-rs/app-server/README.md defines Thread, Turn, Item, start/resume/fork flows, persisted goal state, approval requests, instruction sources, and environment capability fields.

codex-rs/protocol/src/prompts/base_instructions/default.md requires plan use for substantial work, scoped AGENTS.md handling, continued execution until the query is resolved, and concise progress reporting.
codex-rs/skills/src/assets/samples/skill-creator/SKILL.md treats skill description and progressive disclosure as runtime discovery contracts rather than general documentation.

The multi-agent spawn/wait/close handlers and agent registry expose child-agent identity, lifecycle, concurrency/depth limits and explicit resource cleanup; wait returns on terminal targets rather than making standing lanes executable.
codex-rs/core/tests/suite/agent_jobs.rs exercises stable item identity, concurrency, cancellation and result export for batch jobs.
Official Codex Goal guidance assigns /goal to durable work across turns toward a verifiable stopping condition and says to begin with /plan when the desired outcome is still unclear.
upstream_validation: The repository contains the shipped Rust implementation, app-server protocol documentation, schemas, and tests for platform behavior.
A pinned implementation describes the surface but does not prove a Complex convention improves project outcomes.
references.candidate_transfer: Represent every integration as codex_primitive, complex_convention, availability/stability, fallback, and source revision. Use Plan to clarify an unclear path, Goal for a durable objective, child agents as bounded resources with observed IDs and close lifecycle, and standing lanes only as manager-owned responsibility records.
references.rejected: Calling beat_objective or standing lanes Codex primitives, claiming the UI Plan surface changed when it did not, or requesting user permission merely to choose an internal runtime topology.
references.not_transferable: Experimental app-server fields, exact RPCs, and CLI-specific sandbox behavior cannot be promised across every Codex surface.
references.reproduction_result: Not run because the relevant evidence is the shipped app-server and prompt contract; a meaningful comparison requires versioned runs on multiple Codex surfaces.
references.same_task_comparison: One writable codex-exec comparison found both the baseline and candidate completed the same-run queue. The surface exposed no Goal lifecycle trace, so the result is non-discriminating for cross-turn Goal behavior.
references.transfer_artifact: None; public and protocol wording changes remain under the active re-baseline until paired runs are recorded.
references.complex_validation_evidence: None; official platform semantics do not validate Complex behavior.
references.rollback_route: Retain the previous generic capability wording if the more explicit mapping becomes user-visible ceremony without reducing surface errors.
references.refresh_trigger: Codex thread, turn, Goal, approval, skill discovery, subagent, worktree, automation, or Plan documentation changes.
references.next_validation: Run repeated App or instrumented-surface tasks that expose Goal create/resume/complete events, compare them with the prior no-Goal behavior, and obtain independent human outcome review before promotion.

references.original_goal: Provide an agent runtime with tools, handoffs, guardrails, tracing, sessions, and durable human approval at specific sensitive tool calls.
references.original_non_goals: The SDK does not define which project decisions a user must own, and it does not make every internal planning or topology choice approval-bearing.
references.state_or_data_path: A tool declares a boolean or callable approval rule; a pending tool call becomes an interruption with call identity and arguments; RunState stores resolved and unresolved decisions plus resumable runtime context.
references.control_path: The runner evaluates needs_approval at the tool-call boundary, surfaces unresolved ToolApprovalItem entries, serializes RunState when needed, applies approve/reject decisions to exact call IDs, and resumes the original top-level run.
references.operating_constraints: Approval policy depends on the actual tool, arguments, call identity, session persistence, context serialization and application responsibility model; stored state may contain sensitive context.
references.failure_boundaries: Over-broad needs_approval creates unnecessary interruption; approving the wrong call ID or resuming incompatible serialized state can misapply authority; input guardrails do not replace side-effect approval.
references.artifact_revision_provenance: Implementation inspection is pinned to Git commit 95df2c99a745655ba71c763b8ac036283e9df87e; documentation was observed on 2026-07-17 and no SDK package was added to Complex.
implementation_evidence: src/agents/run_internal/approvals.py resolves approval against concrete tool calls and preserves approval state rather than asking generically before planning.
tests/test_runner_guardrail_resume.py exercises interruption and resume behavior across the runner boundary.
examples/tools/shell_human_in_the_loop.py demonstrates approval at a side-effecting shell call instead of treating every agent decision as human-owned.
upstream_validation: The SDK ships approval, serialization and resume tests and documents conditional and programmatic approval paths.
SDK correctness does not validate the Complex responsibility categories or prove fewer unnecessary user interruptions.
references.candidate_transfer: Represent responsibility as decision ownership plus side-effect class; place approval at the exact commitment-bearing operation and preserve call payload, authorization scope, version, idempotency identity and resume route.
references.rejected: Asking the user before reversible internal planning, reading, testing, worker selection or route choice merely because the action is consequential to project progress.
references.not_transferable: Complex is runtime-agnostic and cannot promise Agents SDK ToolApprovalItem, RunState serialization or automatic approval callbacks on every Codex surface.
references.reproduction_result: Not run because importing the SDK approval example would reproduce the upstream runtime, not test the Complex responsibility policy.
references.same_task_comparison: Not run; a staged side-effect harness has not yet compared native Codex approval behavior with the Complex responsibility decision matrix.
references.transfer_artifact: None; the responsibility matrix remains under mechanism revalidation.
references.complex_validation_evidence: None; no real approval or user-intervention comparison is completed.
references.rollback_route: Keep native Codex approval behavior as authority and remove any Complex classification that cannot map to an observed tool-call boundary.
references.refresh_trigger: Agents SDK approval, RunState or Codex approval surfaces change, or a real responsibility-boundary miss appears.
references.next_validation: Run a staged paired experiment with internal reversible actions, exact sensitive calls, persistent resume state and responsibility-holder blind review.

references.original_goal: Maintain a large public technical documentation system with explicit content types, style rules, contribution workflows, previews, localization, and build consistency.
references.original_non_goals: The repository does not define an agent runtime, project control plane, or evidence maturity system for arbitrary research projects.
references.state_or_data_path: Canonical Markdown content, front matter, content-type structure, localized trees, style guidance, build configuration, and review history form the documentation source of truth.
references.control_path: Authors choose the reader task and page type, edit canonical source, run preview/build and style checks, then review through the contribution workflow rather than maintaining parallel prose inventories.
references.operating_constraints: Kubernetes has a large Hugo/Docsy site, release branches, localization teams, and review roles that exceed the needs of a small runtime kit.
references.failure_boundaries: A second manually maintained explanation system can drift from canonical source; mixed tutorial/reference/explanation content can obscure user tasks; style claims without build checks become aspirational.
references.artifact_revision_provenance: Implementation inspection is pinned to Git commit 7ff168ffa18eb581d9cf0cbfb959f81dae6d78fd; no Hugo build or localization workflow is transferred.
implementation_evidence: README.md provides contributor entry, reproducible local/container build paths, and links to content and style guidance.
content/en/docs/contribute/style/content-guide.md separates content purpose and organization instead of treating all pages as one document type.
content/en/docs/contribute/style/style-guide.md specifies direct, reader-oriented language and repository-specific mechanical conventions.
upstream_validation: The live Kubernetes documentation and repository workflow demonstrate sustained multi-contributor operation.
Its scale and governance do not prove the same structure is efficient for Complex.
references.candidate_transfer: Give each public entry an audience and content type; keep README as orientation, quickstart as task guide, core as reference, external methods as explanation, and generate volatile counts or inventories from canonical registries.
references.rejected: Duplicating normative protocol prose in the explainer site, exposing maintenance state as a target-project start step, or importing Kubernetes-specific Hugo/localization machinery.
references.not_transferable: Release-branch, localization, SIG review, and site-generation scale are unnecessary for Complex.
references.reproduction_result: Not run because a full Kubernetes website build would not test the bounded information-architecture transfer.
references.same_task_comparison: Not run; a first-time user study has not compared the old duplicated public surfaces with the canonical-link structure.
references.transfer_artifact: None; the public-surface cleanup remains under the active re-baseline.
references.complex_validation_evidence: None; Kubernetes documentation quality is external design evidence only.
references.rollback_route: Restore a removed explanatory section only if users cannot complete a defined onboarding task from canonical links.
references.refresh_trigger: A new public audience, duplicated normative paragraph, stale generated count, or onboarding failure appears.
references.next_validation: Run a five-minute first-user task against README and quickstart, recording source confusion and completion time.

references.original_goal: Give substantial Rust changes a durable proposal process that records motivation, design, drawbacks, alternatives, unresolved questions, decision state, and implementation follow-through.
references.original_non_goals: The RFC process is not required for routine fixes and does not guarantee that an accepted design succeeds in production.
references.state_or_data_path: A numbered proposal and pull request carry the design record; disposition and tracking links preserve decision state while implementation proceeds elsewhere.
references.control_path: A substantial change is proposed, discussed, revised, accepted/postponed/rejected, then implemented and documented; routine changes bypass the heavy path.
references.operating_constraints: Rust uses community consensus, teams, final-comment periods, and a long-lived RFC archive designed for a language ecosystem.
references.failure_boundaries: Applying the process to every edit creates governance drag; accepting a proposal before implementation evidence can still leave design flaws; unresolved compatibility impact can surface late.
references.artifact_revision_provenance: Implementation inspection is pinned to Git commit f635361c8e3d927208afd6097d4fc5f30e703b43; community governance and archival scale are not transferred.
implementation_evidence: text/0002-rfc-process.md distinguishes substantial from routine changes and records proposal disposition and implementation follow-through.
0000-template.md requires motivation, detailed design, drawbacks, alternatives, unresolved questions, and future possibilities.
README.md makes accepted proposals discoverable without treating the repository as runtime state.
upstream_validation: The repository is Rust's sustained public design-decision record.
Consensus acceptance is governance evidence, not implementation or outcome validation.
references.candidate_transfer: Use one compact change packet only for new public terms, schemas, protocol defaults, surface contracts, or maturity promotions: problem evidence, external implementation, alternatives, drawbacks, compatibility, eval, rollback, owner, and decision status.
references.rejected: Creating an RFC archive, final-comment process, or proposal document for routine wording and local fixes.
references.not_transferable: Community consensus roles, language-governance authority, and historical RFC retention do not fit the active-tree minimalism of Complex.
references.reproduction_result: Not run because the mechanism is a governance workflow; its Complex value requires observing future substantial changes.
references.same_task_comparison: Not run; no mechanism-level Complex change has compared the compact change packet with the previous ad hoc update path.
references.transfer_artifact: None; the active re-baseline defines the first compact decision contract.
references.complex_validation_evidence: None; the Rust process does not validate Complex update quality.
references.rollback_route: Remove the change packet requirement if it becomes routine bureaucracy or duplicates Git review without improving reversibility.
references.refresh_trigger: A substantial protocol/schema/public-term change bypasses alternatives, compatibility, evaluation, or rollback analysis.
references.next_validation: Use the compact packet for the next substantial Complex change and compare review defects and rework with this re-baseline.

references.original_goal: Provide reproducible LLM evaluations with explicit tasks, datasets, solvers, scorers, limits, approvals, logs, retries, metrics, and re-scoring.
references.original_non_goals: Inspect does not define Complex project governance, user responsibility boundaries, or which mechanisms should become protocol core.
references.state_or_data_path: Versioned task definitions, sample inputs, solver/scorer configuration, model/runtime limits, event transcripts, scores, metrics, and eval logs preserve the run and permit later analysis or re-scoring.
references.control_path: Resolve tasks and runtime configuration, execute samples under limits and approval/sandbox policy, log events and results, score with explicit scorers/metrics, and optionally re-score an existing log without rerunning the agent.
references.operating_constraints: Inspect is a Python evaluation runtime with model providers, datasets, sandboxes, concurrency, and rich logs; Complex currently uses lightweight JSON and transcript screens.
references.failure_boundaries: Marker-only checks can be gamed; changing the task, samples, scorer, limits, or model between baseline and candidate invalidates comparison; large logs can violate privacy or active-tree constraints.
references.artifact_revision_provenance: Implementation inspection is pinned to Git commit 8117bf2ff6acc41de137026e2355dec1c0212dfc; no Inspect package is added as a Complex dependency.
implementation_evidence: src/inspect_ai/_eval/task/task.py separates dataset, setup, solver, scorer, metrics, limits, metadata, version, sandbox, approval policy, epochs, checkpoint callbacks, and resume callbacks.
src/inspect_ai/log/_log.py versions EvalLog and separates status, eval specification, plan, results, statistics, errors, samples, reductions, tags, and metadata.
src/inspect_ai/_eval/eval.py records model/runtime configuration, errors, retries, limits, logging, concurrency, and task execution controls.
upstream_validation: The framework includes tests, a large public eval ecosystem, structured logs, and a documented extension model.
Framework capability does not prove a Complex eval is well designed or its scorer agrees with users.
references.candidate_transfer: Separate immutable eval_case, eval_run, and score_record objects; lock case version, samples, scorer, limits, and one changed variable for baseline/candidate comparison; keep private transcripts cold by pointer.
references.rejected: Adding Inspect as a dependency, adopting its scheduler/log format, or treating automated scores as a substitute for human project outcome review.
references.not_transferable: Complex projects may not use Python, model APIs, executable datasets, or public raw transcripts.
references.reproduction_result: Not run because importing Inspect would test the upstream framework rather than the planned lightweight Complex record schema.
references.same_task_comparison: Not run against Inspect; Complex uses a lightweight task/run/score structure, but no equivalent task compares the Inspect runtime with the Complex evaluator.
references.transfer_artifact: None; docs/evals/complex-eval-record.schema.json and docs/evals/experiment-program.json are independently implemented candidate artifacts inspired by the inspected structure, not a transferred Inspect runtime.
references.complex_validation_evidence: None; Inspect's evaluation maturity is external design evidence only.
references.rollback_route: Keep the current compact human review record if the three-object schema adds burden without improving comparison integrity.
references.refresh_trigger: A maturity claim, scorer change, Inspect log/task contract change, or real transcript batch occurs.
references.next_validation: Apply the locked task/run/score record to a redacted real-project comparison and test whether an independent reviewer can reproduce the decision.
